content.text: HTML forms are the primary mechanism through which users send data to a server — login credentials, search queries, contact messages, file uploads, settings changes. Every form element is either a
container, a control that collects a value (
). Understanding what each piece does — and how Next.js can receive the submitted data — is essential for building any interactive application.

attrs.alt: HTML form anatomy: form element wrapping fieldset, label+input pairs, textarea, select, and submit button with annotations

content.text: element wraps all of the controls that belong to one submission. It has two critical attributes:
Where form data is sent on submit. Defaults to the current page URL.
GET appends data to the URL as query params; POST sends data in the request body.
Encoding for POST. Use multipart/form-data for file uploads.
Disables browser built-in validation. Useful when you handle validation in JavaScript.

<!-- GET form — data appears in URL: /search?q=nextjs -->
<form action="/search" method="get">
  <label for="q">Search</label>
  <input type="text" id="q" name="q">
  <button type="submit">Go</button>
</form>

<!-- POST form — data sent in request body, not visible in URL -->
<form action="/contact" method="post">
  <label for="email">Email</label>
  <input type="email" id="email" name="email" required>
  <button type="submit">Subscribe</button>
</form>

<!-- File upload — must use POST + multipart/form-data -->
<form action="/upload" method="post" enctype="multipart/form-data">
  <label for="avatar">Profile photo</label>
  <input type="file" id="avatar" name="avatar" accept="image/*">
  <button type="submit">Upload</button>
</form>
for search and filter forms — the URL becomes bookmarkable and shareable. Use
for any form that creates, modifies, or deletes data, or that sends sensitive information like passwords.
Every form control must have an associated
. A label is not optional — it is required for accessibility. Screen readers announce the label text when the user focuses the control, and clicking the label transfers focus to the input.
There are two ways to associate a label with a control:

<!-- Method 1: for/id association (most common) -->
<label for="username">Username</label>
<input type="text" id="username" name="username">

<!-- Method 2: wrapping label (implicit association) -->
<label>
  Password
  <input type="password" name="password">
</label>
attribute on the input. In JSX (React/Next.js),
Never use placeholder text as a substitute for a label. Placeholders disappear when the user types, leaving them with no reminder of what the field expects.

controls three things: the validation rules the browser applies, the virtual keyboard shown on mobile, and the visual control rendered. Using the correct type is both a usability and accessibility concern.

<!-- text: single-line free text (default) -->
<input type="text" name="username" placeholder="e.g. alice123">

<!-- email: validates email format; mobile shows @ keyboard -->
<input type="email" name="email" autocomplete="email">

<!-- password: masks characters; triggers password manager -->
<input type="password" name="password" autocomplete="current-password">

<!-- search: styled as a search box; may add clear button -->
<input type="search" name="q" placeholder="Search...">

<!-- url: validates URL format -->
<input type="url" name="website" placeholder="https://example.com">

<!-- tel: shows numeric keypad on mobile (no format validation) -->
<input type="tel" name="phone" placeholder="+1 555 000 0000">

<!-- number: validates numeric input; shows spinner arrows -->
<input type="number" name="quantity" min="1" max="99" step="1" value="1">

<!-- range: slider control; does not show the current value by default -->
<input type="range" name="volume" min="0" max="100" step="5" value="50">

<!-- date: date picker (YYYY-MM-DD) -->
<input type="date" name="birthday" min="1900-01-01" max="2025-12-31">

<!-- datetime-local: date + time picker (no timezone) -->
<input type="datetime-local" name="appointment">

<!-- month: month picker (YYYY-MM) -->
<input type="month" name="expiry">

<!-- time: time picker (HH:MM) -->
<input type="time" name="start-time" step="900">  <!-- 15-min steps -->

<!-- checkbox: on/off toggle; name=value is sent only when checked -->
<input type="checkbox" id="terms" name="terms" value="agreed">
<label for="terms">I agree to the terms of service</label>

<!-- Multiple checkboxes with the same name become an array on the server -->
<fieldset>
  <legend>Interests</legend>
  <label><input type="checkbox" name="interests" value="html"> HTML</label>
  <label><input type="checkbox" name="interests" value="css"> CSS</label>
  <label><input type="checkbox" name="interests" value="js"> JavaScript</label>
</fieldset>

<!-- radio: one choice from a group; same name = mutually exclusive -->
<fieldset>
  <legend>Preferred contact method</legend>
  <label><input type="radio" name="contact" value="email"> Email</label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="contact" value="phone"> Phone</label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="contact" value="post"> Post</label>
</fieldset>
form a group — selecting one deselects the others. Each group should be wrapped in a
that names the group. This ensures screen readers announce the group context ("Preferred contact method") before announcing each option.

<!-- file: opens OS file picker -->
<input type="file" name="resume" accept=".pdf,.doc,.docx" multiple>

<!-- hidden: sends data to server without user interaction -->
<input type="hidden" name="csrf_token" value="abc123xyz">
<input type="hidden" name="referrer" value="email_campaign">

<!-- submit: submits the form -->
<input type="submit" value="Send Message">

<!-- button as submit (more flexible — can contain HTML) -->
<button type="submit">Send Message</button>

<!-- button type=button: does NOT submit the form -->
<button type="button" onclick="preview()">Preview</button>

<!-- reset: resets all controls to initial values (rarely useful) -->
<button type="reset">Clear form</button>
, it has an explicit closing tag and its default content goes between the tags (not in a

<label for="message">Message</label>
<textarea
  id="message"
  name="message"
  rows="6"
  cols="50"
  maxlength="2000"
  placeholder="Describe your issue..."
  required
></textarea>
attribute sets the initial visible height. Use CSS
to control whether the user can resize it (
element renders a dropdown list of

<label for="country">Country</label>
<select id="country" name="country" required>
  <option value="">-- Select a country --</option>
  <optgroup label="North America">
    <option value="us">United States</option>
    <option value="ca">Canada</option>
    <option value="mx">Mexico</option>
  </optgroup>
  <optgroup label="Europe">
    <option value="gb">United Kingdom</option>
    <option value="de">Germany</option>
    <option value="fr">France</option>
  </optgroup>
</select>

<!-- Multiple selection -->
<label for="skills">Skills (hold Ctrl/Cmd to multi-select)</label>
<select id="skills" name="skills" multiple size="5">
  <option value="html">HTML</option>
  <option value="css">CSS</option>
  <option value="js">JavaScript</option>
  <option value="react">React</option>
  <option value="nextjs" selected>Next.js</option>
</select>
. This is especially important for radio button and checkbox groups, where the group name provides the context needed to understand each option:

<form action="/register" method="post">
  <fieldset>
    <legend>Account Information</legend>
    <label for="reg-email">Email</label>
    <input type="email" id="reg-email" name="email" required>

    <label for="reg-pass">Password</label>
    <input type="password" id="reg-pass" name="password"
           minlength="8" required>
  </fieldset>

  <fieldset>
    <legend>Notification Preferences</legend>
    <label>
      <input type="checkbox" name="notify" value="product"> Product updates
    </label>
    <label>
      <input type="checkbox" name="notify" value="marketing"> Marketing emails
    </label>
    <label>
      <input type="checkbox" name="notify" value="security" checked> Security alerts
    </label>
  </fieldset>

  <button type="submit">Create account</button>
</form>
HTML5 provides several attributes that trigger browser-native validation before submission:
Field cannot be empty on submit

<!-- Required with minlength -->
<label for="username">Username (3-20 characters)</label>
<input type="text" id="username" name="username"
       required minlength="3" maxlength="20"
       pattern="[a-zA-Z0-9_]+"
       title="Letters, numbers, and underscores only">

<!-- Number with range -->
<label for="age">Age (18-120)</label>
<input type="number" id="age" name="age" min="18" max="120" required>

<!-- Pattern matching -->
<label for="zip">US ZIP code</label>
<input type="text" id="zip" name="zip"
       pattern="[0-9]{5}(-[0-9]{4})?"
       title="5-digit ZIP code, optionally followed by a dash and 4 digits"
       placeholder="90210">
Browser validation runs before the form submits. If a control is invalid, the browser focuses it and shows a tooltip. The
attribute becomes the error message in most browsers. This is free input protection — use it. You will still validate on the server, but these constraints prevent many accidental submissions.
— async functions that run on the server and can be called directly from a form's
directive at the top of the function body marks it as a Server Action. Next.js handles routing the form submission to the server and calling the function with the submitted
. The form works even with JavaScript disabled — it degrades gracefully to a standard HTML form submission.
To show a loading state during submission, use the
hook (React DOM) inside a child component of the form:
In the Pages Router, form data is typically sent to an API route via
In React, "controlled" inputs store their value in state so the component always knows the current form values — useful for conditional rendering, live validation, or computed fields:
ensures screen readers announce the error when it appears. Place errors adjacent to the field they describe, not in a single block at the top of the form.

<!-- ❌ No label — screen readers announce only the type and value -->
<input type="email" placeholder="Enter your email">

<!-- ✅ Explicit label -->
<label for="email">Email address</label>
<input type="email" id="email" name="email" placeholder="you@example.com">
Using placeholder as the only label

<!-- ❌ Placeholder disappears on input — user forgets what the field is -->
<input type="text" placeholder="Full name" name="name">

<!-- ✅ Label always visible; placeholder provides an example -->
<label for="name">Full name</label>
<input type="text" id="name" name="name" placeholder="e.g. Alice Johnson">

<!-- ❌ type="text" for email: no validation, no @ keyboard on mobile -->
<input type="text" name="email">

<!-- ✅ type="email": browser validates format, shows optimized keyboard -->
<input type="email" name="email" autocomplete="email">

<!-- ❌ type="text" for password: characters visible -->
<input type="text" name="password">

<!-- ✅ type="password": characters masked, triggers password manager -->
<input type="password" name="password" autocomplete="current-password">

<!-- ❌ No name — this field will NOT be included in form submission -->
<input type="text" id="city" placeholder="City">

<!-- ✅ name is the key in the submitted form data -->
<input type="text" id="city" name="city" placeholder="City">
Calling preventDefault unnecessarily on Server Action forms
or by wrapping. No exceptions for accessibility.
